title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
description: Master the core concepts of digital images, color spaces, and edge detection through interactive lessons and assessments.

nodes.title: What are Digital Images?
nodes.content: Digital images are composed of discrete elements called pixels, each representing a small color sample. Images are typically represented using color spaces like RGB (Red, Green, Blue), where each pixel has values for intensity of red, green, and blue light. Understanding pixel representation is fundamental to computer vision, as all image processing operations manipulate these pixel values. Color spaces help us interpret and manipulate colors in different ways, depending on the application. The ability to access and modify individual pixel values forms the foundation for all computer vision techniques.

nodes.title: Understanding RGB Values
nodes.content: Consider a 3x3 pixel image. The top-left pixel has RGB values (255, 0, 0), representing pure red. The center pixel has (128, 128, 128), a neutral gray. The bottom-right pixel has (0, 255, 0), representing pure green. In a grayscale image, the same RGB value is used for all three channels. Each value ranges from 0 (no intensity) to 255 (maximum intensity). By understanding these values, we can write algorithms to detect colors, adjust brightness, or apply filters.

nodes.title: Image Representation Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of digital image representation and color spaces.

questions.question: In the RGB color space, what RGB values represent pure white?
feedback: Correct! All channels at maximum = white.
questions.explanation: In RGB, white is when all three color channels are at their maximum value of 255.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of color spaces in digital image processing?
options: To provide different ways of representing and manipulating colors
feedback: Correct! Color spaces provide different representations for different tasks.
questions.explanation: Color spaces like RGB, HSV, and LAB allow us to represent and manipulate colors in ways suited to different applications.

nodes.title: What would you like to explore next?
nodes.content: Excellent work! You understand image basics. Now you can either continue learning about edge detection—one of the most powerful techniques in computer vision—or complete the course and explore more topics later.

nodes.title: Edge Detection Fundamentals
nodes.content: Edges are transitions in pixel intensity that mark boundaries between objects and regions. Edge detection algorithms identify these transitions automatically, forming the basis for object recognition, image segmentation, and feature extraction. The Sobel operator and Canny edge detector are two popular algorithms that apply mathematical filters to highlight edges. These filters work by computing gradients—measuring how quickly pixel intensity changes—in different directions. Detecting edges effectively requires balancing sensitivity (catching all edges) with specificity (avoiding false positives from noise).

nodes.title: The Sobel Edge Detector in Action
nodes.content: The Sobel operator uses two 3x3 kernels to detect edges in horizontal and vertical directions. When applied to an image of a hand, the algorithm computes gradients at each pixel, producing high values where intensity changes rapidly (at object boundaries) and low values in uniform regions. The vertical Sobel kernel emphasizes left-right edges, while the horizontal kernel emphasizes up-down edges. Combining both kernels reveals all edges in the image. By adjusting the threshold for what constitutes an 'edge,' you control the detector's sensitivity to subtle or noisy transitions.

nodes.title: Edge Detection Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of edge detection concepts and algorithms.

questions.question: What does an edge in an image represent?
options: A sudden change in color or brightness
The border of the image frame
A straight line in the image
questions.explanation: Edges are defined as rapid transitions in pixel intensity values, typically at object boundaries.

questions.question: Why is it important to adjust the threshold when using edge detectors?
options: To control sensitivity and avoid detecting noise as edges
feedback: Correct! Threshold controls what counts as an edge.
questions.explanation: Threshold adjustment balances finding true edges while rejecting noise-induced false positives.

nodes.title: Practice: Reinforcing Concepts
nodes.content: Take your time with these practice questions to solidify your understanding of image representation, pixel values, and edge detection algorithms.

questions.question: What is a pixel?
options: A small square region of color in a digital image
feedback: Correct! Pixels are the smallest unit of a digital image.
That's not what we mean by pixel.
questions.explanation: A pixel is the smallest discrete element of a digital image, containing color information.

questions.question: Which algorithm is known for using gradients to detect edges?
questions.explanation: The Sobel operator is one of the most popular gradient-based edge detection methods in computer vision.

nodes.title: Congratulations!
nodes.content: You've completed Computer Vision Fundamentals! You now understand digital image representation, color spaces, and edge detection techniques. These foundational concepts form the basis for advanced computer vision tasks like object detection, image segmentation, and feature matching. Next steps: implement these algorithms using OpenCV or scikit-image, experiment with real images, and explore more specialized topics like feature descriptors, optical flow, or deep learning-based vision. Keep building and learning!
